examples.title: Process Creation using fork()
examples.description: Demonstrates creating a new process in Unix-like systems using fork() system call
examples.explanation: The fork() system call creates an exact copy of the parent process. It returns twice: to the parent it returns the child's PID, and to the child it returns 0. The wait() call ensures the parent waits for the child to finish execution.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is the size of parent process memory copied

examples.title: Process States Simulation
examples.description: Demonstrates process states and transitions in a simplified operating system simulation
examples.code:
import time
import random

class Process:
    def __init__(self, pid):
        self.pid = pid
        self.state = "NEW"
        self.ProgramCounter = 0
    
    def admit(self):
        self.state = "READY"
        print(f"Process {self.pid}: NEW -> READY")
    
    def dispatch(self):
        self.state = "RUNNING"
        print(f"Process {self.pid}: READY -> RUNNING")
    
    def preempt(self):
        self.state = "READY"
        print(f"Process {self.pid}: RUNNING -> READY (preempt)")
    
    def wait_for_io(self):
        self.state = "WAITING"
        print(f"Process {self.pid}: RUNNING -> WAITING (I/O)")
    
    def io_complete(self):
        self.state = "READY"
        print(f"Process {self.pid}: WAITING -> READY (I/O done)")
    
    def terminate(self):
        self.state = "TERMINATED"
        print(f"Process {self.pid}: RUNNING -> TERMINATED")

# Simulation
p = Process(1001)
p.admit()
p.dispatch()

if random.random() > 0.5:
    p.wait_for_io()
    time.sleep(1)
    p.io_complete()
else:
    p.preempt()

p.dispatch()
p.terminate()
examples.explanation: This Python code simulates the process state transitions in an operating system. It demonstrates how a process moves through different states: NEW -> READY -> RUNNING -> (either WAITING or READY) -> TERMINATED, showing the dynamic nature of process management.

examples.title: Context Switching Algorithm
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how context switching works between two processes
examples.code:
// Data structures
PCB1: Process Control Block for Process 1
PCB2: Process Control Block for Process 2

// Context Switch Procedure
PROCEDURE ContextSwitch(currentProcess, nextProcess)
BEGIN
    // Step 1: Save current process context
    PCB1.state = READY
    PCB1.ProgramCounter = CPU.PC
    PCB1.CPU_Registers = CPU.RegisterSet
    PCB1.StackPointer = CPU.StackPtr
    
    // Step 2: Update scheduling queues
    AddToReadyQueue(PCB1)
    
    // Step 3: Select next process
    nextProcess = SelectFromReadyQueue()
    
    // Step 4: Remove next process from queue
    RemoveFromReadyQueue(nextProcess)
    
    // Step 5: Restore next process context
    nextProcess.state = RUNNING
    CPU.PC = nextProcess.ProgramCounter
    CPU.RegisterSet = nextProcess.CPU_Registers
    CPU.StackPtr = nextProcess.StackPointer
    
    // Step 6: Begin execution
    JUMP_TO(CPU.PC)
END

// Main Execution Loop
WHILE systemRunning DO
    currentProcess = GetRunningProcess()
    nextProcess = SelectFromReadyQueue()
    
    IF nextProcess != NULL THEN
        ContextSwitch(currentProcess, nextProcess)
    END IF
END WHILE
examples.explanation: Context switching involves saving the complete state of the currently running process into its PCB and loading the state of another process from its PCB. This includes saving all CPU registers, program counter, stack pointer, and other execution context. The scheduler decides which process to run next, and the dispatcher performs the actual context switch.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for each save/restore operation, but total overhead depends on number of registers and system architecture
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of processes in the system for PCB storage
